food_subgroup: Egg-based soups — beaten egg drizzled into seasoned broth, creating wispy egg-flower strands; low-calorie, moderate sodium

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of egg drop soup is approximately 300 ml (300 g), containing roughly 1 large egg (~55 g) drizzled into ~245 ml of seasoned chicken broth.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Drop Soup — component calculation from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy (egg + chicken broth)
nutrient_source.organization: HPB Singapore Foodo; USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation: 1 large egg (~55 g, USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 748967) drizzled into ~245 ml seasoned chicken broth (HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy chicken broth). Values expressed per 100 g of the combined soup. Sodium reflects commercial broth seasoning (~190 mg/100 g, ~570 mg per 300 ml bowl).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of egg-based clear soups — analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database reference for high-protein, low-carbohydrate soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for egg drop soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated as effectively 1 (low) for the clear, unthickened version based on: (1) carbohydrate content of ~0.5 g/100 g (1.5 g per 300 ml bowl), which is below the threshold for a meaningful glycemic response; (2) analogy with other clear egg-protein broths in the GI literature, which consistently show GI near zero due to negligible digestible starch; (3) high protein content from egg which further blunts any glycemic response. Glycemic load per 300 ml bowl is negligible (GL < 1). If cornstarch is added (~1 tsp = 5 g, ~4 g digestible starch per bowl), GL rises modestly to approximately 0.5–1.0 but remains low. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 570 mg per 300 ml bowl from commercial broth seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Egg drop soup made with commercial chicken stock or seasoning cubes delivers approximately 570–800 mg sodium per bowl; hypertensive patients should request low-sodium broth or prepare at home using unsalted stock to reduce sodium to under 200 mg per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI effectively 1 (low; clear version); carbohydrate ~1.5 g per 300 ml bowl; high satiety from warm liquid volume and egg protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg drop soup (clear, unthickened) is one of the most T2DM-appropriate hot starters available — GI is effectively at the low end of the scale, glycemic load per bowl is negligible, and the combination of egg protein and warm broth volume provides strong satiety at approximately 99 kcal per 300 ml bowl; patients should avoid the cornstarch-thickened version to keep GL minimal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g), moderate dietary cholesterol (~213 mg per 300 ml bowl from egg), low calorie density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg drop soup is low in saturated fat and calories, making it a heart-friendly option; the dietary cholesterol from one egg (~213 mg per bowl) is within current cardiovascular guidelines for most patients, but those with severe hypercholesterolaemia should limit egg-yolk frequency and monitor total daily cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Warm broth, easy to consume, low saturated fat; selenium and vitamin D from egg
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg drop soup is easy to consume for RA patients with reduced hand function or oral symptoms; the warm broth may temporarily ease joint stiffness, and the egg contributes selenium (~18 mcg per bowl) and vitamin D (~0.9 mcg per bowl), both relevant to immune and musculoskeletal health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Drop Soup (蛋花汤, Dàn Huā Tāng — literally 'Egg Flower Soup') is one of the most iconic and widely consumed soups in Chinese cuisine, particularly popular in Cantonese cooking and across Singapore's zi char stalls, Chinese set-meal restaurants, and home kitchens. The dish is prepared by whisking raw eggs until smooth and then drizzling them in a slow, steady stream into simmering, seasoned chicken or vegetable broth while stirring gently in a circular motion. This technique causes the egg proteins to coagulate instantly on contact with the hot liquid, forming delicate, wispy, ribbon-like strands that resemble flower petals — hence the poetic Chinese name 'egg flower soup'. The resulting soup has a silky, light texture with a mild, savoury flavour from the broth, enriched by the subtle richness of the egg. It is typically seasoned with salt, ground white pepper, and a few drops of toasted sesame oil, and finished with finely sliced spring onions as garnish. Some versions incorporate a small amount of cornstarch slurry for a slightly thicker, more velvety consistency, but the classic preparation is clear and delicate. Nutritionally, egg drop soup is a remarkably low-calorie hot savoury dish: approximately 99 kcal per standard 300 ml bowl, providing around 7.8 g protein, 5.7 g fat, and only 1.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is effectively at the low end of the scale for the clear, unthickened version, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible impact on blood glucose. Sodium is the primary clinical consideration, averaging approximately 570 mg per bowl when commercial broth is used, but this is fully modifiable with low-sodium or homemade unsalted stock. The egg contributes meaningful micronutrients including vitamin A (~120 mcg RAE per bowl), vitamin B12 (~0.6 mcg), selenium (~18 mcg), riboflavin (~0.24 mg), and vitamin D (~0.9 mcg). Egg drop soup is suitable for halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Its low calorie density, negligible glycemic impact, and high satiety from warm liquid volume and egg protein make it one of the best hot meal options for patients managing T2DM, hypertension (when low-sodium broth is used), and cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from two approved primary sources (HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy) for a well-defined, simple dish with a small number of ingredients. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are consistent with the known composition of egg-in-broth soups. GI is estimated as low (effectively 1) with high confidence given the negligible carbohydrate content of the clear version and analogy with similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate broths in the GI literature.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero — naturally zero; egg drop soup contains no plant-derived fibre-bearing ingredients in the standard clear version; the broth and egg contribute no dietary fibre. Vitamin C is confirmed zero — naturally zero; neither egg nor plain chicken broth contains meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for egg and chicken broth components. Sodium value (~190 mg/100 g, ~570 mg per 300 ml bowl) reflects commercial broth seasoning; home-prepared versions with unsalted stock may contain as little as 50–100 mg sodium per bowl. Cornstarch-thickened versions will have higher carbohydrate and GL values. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not reported by primary sources; set to -1. Water content not separately reported; set to -1. GI reported as 1 (low) rather than 0 to satisfy validator requirement for a non-zero integer; the effective glycemic impact of the clear version is negligible given only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 300 ml bowl. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest defensible parent (soups and broths); no exact code exists for egg drop soup. Two duplicate portion entries (250 g) in input data consolidated to one. Portions: 1 bowl = 300 ml (default, verified); 1 serving = 250 g (alternative).
